# A2.3 Data Transmissions


## A2.3.1 Types of IP Addressing


### IPv4 and IPv6 Addressing


IP addressing is the backbone of internet communication, and understanding the two main versions is crucial for any computer scientist.

#### IPv4


  * 32-bit address format (4 bytes)
  * Written as four octets separated by dots (e.g., 192.168.1.1)
  * Supports approximately 4.3 billion unique addresses

<callout type=""></callout>

#### IPv6


  * 128-bit address format (16 bytes)
  * Written in hexadecimal, separated by colons (e.g., 2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334)
  * Supports approximately 340 undecillion unique addresses

<callout type=""></callout>

### Public and Private IP Addresses


#### Public IP Addresses


  * Globally unique addresses
  * Directly accessible over the internet
  * Assigned by Internet Service Providers (ISPs)

#### Private IP Addresses


Reserved ranges for internal networks:

  * 10.0.0.0 to 10.255.255.255
  * 172.16.0.0 to 172.31.255.255
  * 192.168.0.0 to 192.168.255.255

<callout type=""></callout>

### Static vs Dynamic IP Addresses


#### Static IP Addresses


  * Manually configured
  * Never change
  * Ideal for servers and network infrastructure

#### Dynamic IP Addresses


  * Automatically assigned by DHCP
  * Can change over time
  * More efficient for resource management

## A2.3.2 Transmission Media


### Wired Transmission


#### Fiber Optic Cables


  * Uses light to transmit data
  * Extremely high bandwidth (100+ Gbps)
  * Immune to electromagnetic interference
  * Low signal loss over long distances

<callout type=""></callout>

#### Twisted Pair Cables


  * Cat5e, Cat6, Cat7 standards
  * Bandwidth varies (1-10 Gbps)
  * More susceptible to interference
  * Cost-effective solution

### Wireless Transmission


  * Uses radio waves or microwaves
  * Standards include WiFi (802.11)
  * Variable speeds depending on technology
  * Affected by physical obstacles and interference

<callout type=""></callout>

## A2.3.3 Data Packet Transmission


### Packet Switching


Data is broken into smaller packets for transmission:

  1. Source breaks data into packets
  2. Each packet contains header information
  3. Packets can take different routes
  4. Destination reassembles packets

<callout type=""></callout>

### Data Packet Structure


A typical packet includes:

  * Header (source/destination addresses)
  * Payload (actual data)
  * Trailer (error checking)

## A2.3.4 Network Protocols


### Transport Layer Protocols


#### TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)


  * Connection-oriented
  * Guaranteed delivery
  * Error checking and correction
  * Flow control

#### UDP (User Datagram Protocol)


  * Connectionless
  * No guarantee of delivery
  * Faster than TCP
  * Used for real-time applications

<callout type=""></callout>
